does: This paper develops a Cross-Agent Multimodal Provenance-Aware Defense Framework that protects agentic AI systems from prompt injection attacks across multiple modalities. The framework implements three specialized components—a text sanitizer agent, visual sanitizer agent, and output validator agent—all coordinated by a provenance ledger that tracks metadata about modality, source, and trust level. By sanitizing both user-generated and upstream agent-produced prompts and independently verifying LLM outputs before they reach downstream nodes, the approach aims to prevent malicious instructions embedded in text, images, metadata, or inter-agent messages from propagating through agent graphs and causing unintended behavior, policy violations, or state corruption in autonomous systems.
connects: This work extends prior research on prompt injection vulnerabilities and adversarial attacks against language models by addressing the emerging threat landscape of multimodal agentic systems. It builds on foundational work in LLM security and robustness, while responding to specific challenges introduced by systems like LangChain and GraphChain that integrate multiple specialized agents. The paper connects to broader streams of research on input validation, content moderation, and provenance tracking in distributed systems. It also engages with growing literature on vision-language model vulnerabilities and the security implications of tool-use in autonomous agents, combining insights from prompt robustness, multimodal security, and supply-chain integrity approaches.
enables: This framework enables the deployment of more trustworthy autonomous agentic systems by establishing defenses against a critical attack surface that grows with system complexity. The provenance-aware approach opens pathways for formal verification of agent interactions and could support certification of agentic AI safety in high-stakes domains. Follow-up work could develop stronger sanitization techniques resistant to adversarial evasion, extend the framework to emerging modalities, and establish quantitative metrics for measuring prompt injection robustness. The provenance ledger infrastructure could enable auditing of agent decision chains and support human oversight mechanisms, potentially facilitating more transparent and controllable multi-agent systems suitable for safety-critical applications.
